preFix.title: Carved maple sauce spoon
preFix.subtitle: A small deep-bowled spoon for sauces and dressings
preFix.excerpt: A small green-maple sauce spoon with a deep narrow bowl and a short handle. About 16 cm finished length. The proportions suit scraping a pan, stirring a roux, or spooning dressing. Good first project for building hook-knife control.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for the axe-and-knife blank method. Small treen documented in Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861).

content.text: A sauce spoon is small enough that you can rough out the blank with a
sloyd knife

alone if the starting billet is 20 mm thick or less. Maple is excellent for this: close-grained, hard enough to take a clean surface finish, food-safe when oiled, and available from most hardwood suppliers. A piece of
green wood
18 cm long and 4 cm wide is enough.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

Maple is harder than sycamore when green but still carves cleanly under a sharp edge. The tight grain polishes to a smooth surface that is easy to clean. It dries food-safe and pale. Field maple (Acer campestre), the native British species, is excellent. Sycamore (Acer pseudoplatanus) is the next best option and behaves almost identically. Norwegian maple and sugar maple both work well.
Shaping the outer profile

Draw the outline on your billet: a round bowl about 4 cm across and 12 mm thick at the shallowest, and a short handle 12 cm long tapering to 10 mm. Use push cuts with the sloyd knife to remove wood from both sides of the handle, leaving the bowl section proud. Work from the neck of the handle toward the tip; the grain runs along the length so these cuts travel with the grain and produce clean, thin shavings.

Shape the back of the bowl to a shallow dome. Push cuts from the widest point of the bowl toward the neck and toward the tip. A stop cut at the neck boundary prevents the cut from running into the handle section.
Hollowing

The small hook knife is the right tool for a bowl this size. Work in short arcs from the centre outward. Maple is harder than sycamore; the edge needs to be sharper to cut cleanly. Strop before starting the hollow and again every ten minutes.
The finished bowl depth is 8 to 10 mm. The wall thickness at the rim is 3 mm. Check the rim by pressing your thumbnail against it; 3 mm is about the width of a doubled fingernail.
Drying and finishing
Dry in a paper bag for two to three weeks. Sand with 400 grit, then oil with raw linseed oil. Wipe the surplus after one hour. Two weeks before food contact.
Variations
Angled tip: taper the front rim of the bowl at 30 degrees to create a straight cutting edge. Useful for scraping the fond from a pan without scratching a non-stick surface.
Sycamore in place of maple: slightly softer and easier on a beginner's edge, same food-safe properties.
Care
Hand wash and towel-dry. Re-oil with a food-safe oil once or twice a year.

rows.fix: Hone on the 1000-grit waterstone, then strop. Maple needs a finer edge than sycamore.
rows.cause: Edge not sharp enough for maple's harder grain
rows.symptom: Hook knife skids on the surface rather than cutting

rows.fix: Use your fingertip to check rim thickness all the way round before each session. Mark the thick areas with a pencil and take from those only.
rows.symptom: Rim very thin on one side, thick on the other

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
